question: A patient with biopsy-proven primary lung carcinoma treated with chemoradiation 6 months ago undergoes abdominal CT as part of further evaluation. Based on the imaging, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the images. The top axial image of the chest shows a lobulated soft-tissue mass in the left apical/mediastinal region (yellow arrow), consistent with the known, biopsy-proven primary lung carcinoma stated in the question. The bottom axial abdominal CT shows a discrete, round soft-tissue mass (yellow arrow) in the right upper quadrant/upper midline region adjacent to the second portion of the duodenum and pancreatic head region; both kidneys are visible and the lesion lies anterior to the vertebral body and medial to the right kidney.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical question, which asks the most likely diagnosis given a patient with recently treated primary lung cancer undergoing abdominal CT as part of further evaluation. The abdominal lesion appears as a well-defined solid soft-tissue nodule separate from the renal parenchyma and not clearly intraparenchymal within the pancreas or projecting into a bowel lumen.

Then, I consider how each option lines up with these observations. A metastatic deposit from the known lung carcinoma (option A) fits the clinical context (staging/follow-up imaging after lung cancer) and the imaging appearance of a discrete solid soft-tissue nodule in the peripancreatic/duodenal region. A primary duodenal adenocarcinoma (B) would be expected to arise from and involve the bowel wall/duodenal lumen, which is not seen here. A GIST (C) would usually demonstrate a clear origin from the gastric or small-bowel wall with an exophytic component; this lesion does not show an obvious contiguous bowel wall origin. A pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor (D) would be expected to be within the pancreatic parenchyma; the lesion here appears separate from the pancreas rather than intraparenchymal.

Therefore, integrating the imaging appearance (discrete peripancreatic/duodenal-region soft-tissue nodule not clearly arising from pancreas or bowel) with the clinical history of known lung carcinoma under evaluation for metastases, the most likely diagnosis is metastatic lung carcinoma.
caption: A CT scan of the chest from the patient's prior hospitalization (top image) showing a large, lobulated, left apical lung mass (arrow). Transthoracic FNA results later came back positive for poorly differentiated lung carcinoma with focal neuroendocrine differentiation. The bottom image is from a CT scan of the abdomen that was done on presentation, which shows a large elliptical lesion (arrow) around the third portion of the duodenum, later found on esophagogastroduodenoscopy, whose biopsies were consistent with metastatic lung carcinoma.
sub_caption: The bottom image is from a CT scan of the abdomen that was done on presentation, which shows a large elliptical lesion (arrow) around the third portion of the duodenum, later found on esophagogastroduodenoscopy, whose biopsies were consistent with metastatic lung carcinoma.
image_context: Of note, the patient was recently diagnosed with lung cancer 6 months prior to his presentation. At that time, he was complaining of chronic hemoptysis, unintentional 15-pound weight loss, and worsening fatigue over a 6-month time period. Prior chest X-rays were negative; however, a computerized tomography (CT) scan of the chest showed a new left-sided lobulated, apical lesion measuring 4 × 2 cm with bronchial involvement, and hilar lymphadenopathy (Fig. <xref ref-type="fig" rid="crg-0014-0186-g01">1</xref>). The patient was worked up for an infectious source, including tuberculosis, which ended up being negative. He had a bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage and a transbronchial biopsy done, which showed inflammatory cells but was negative for any malignant cells on brushing or washing. However, due to a high suspicion for malignancy, the patient ultimately had a transthoracic fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the mass. The final pathology results demonstrated poorly differentiated carcinoma that was weakly positive (only a few scattered cells) for CK-7, synaptophysin, and calretinin on IHC staining (Fig. ). The patient was worked up for an infectious source, including tuberculosis, which ended up being negative. He had a bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage and a transbronchial biopsy done, which showed inflammatory cells but was negative for any malignant cells on brushing or washing. However, due to a high suspicion for malignancy, the patient ultimately had a transthoracic fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of the mass. The final pathology results demonstrated poorly differentiated carcinoma that was weakly positive (only a few scattered cells) for CK-7, synaptophysin, and calretinin on IHC staining (Fig. <xref ref-type="fig" rid="crg-0014-0186-g02">2</xref>). IHC staining of the specimen was also negative for CK-20, p63, TTF-1, chromogranin, melan-A, CDX-2, PSA, and PSMA (Fig. ). IHC staining of the specimen was also negative for CK-20, p63, TTF-1, chromogranin, melan-A, CDX-2, PSA, and PSMA (Fig. <xref ref-type="fig" rid="crg-0014-0186-g02">2</xref>). The patient was further worked up for his malignancy with a magnetic resonance imaging scan of the brain which was negative for any lesions. However, a positron emission tomography scan was done and found an active left upper-lobe apical mass as well as hypermetabolic mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, which were compatible with metastatic disease. His pretreatment stage at the time was determined to be T4N2M0, stage IIIB. He was started on a paclitaxel/carboplatin chemotherapy regimen with concurrent radiation therapy (total of 6,000 cgy) and was in the process of planning a surgical resection evaluation of the lung tumor prior to his hospitalization.). The patient was further worked up for his malignancy with a magnetic resonance imaging scan of the brain which was negative for any lesions. However, a positron emission tomography scan was done and found an active left upper-lobe apical mass as well as hypermetabolic mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, which were compatible with metastatic disease. His pretreatment stage at the time was determined to be T4N2M0, stage IIIB. He was started on a paclitaxel/carboplatin chemotherapy regimen with concurrent radiation therapy (total of 6,000 cgy) and was in the process of planning a surgical resection evaluation of the lung tumor prior to his hospitalization.
relevant_image_context: The bottom image is from a CT scan of the abdomen that was done on presentation, which shows a large elliptical lesion (arrow) around the third portion of the duodenum, later found on esophagogastroduodenoscopy, whose biopsies were consistent with metastatic lung carcinoma.

knowledge.claim_text: New discrete solid soft-tissue nodules on staging or follow-up imaging are typically associated with metastatic deposits in patients with a known primary malignancy.

knowledge.claim_text: A lesion that is separate from an organ's parenchyma or is not intraparenchymal is typically less likely to originate from that organ.
